title: The 1998 Movie Draft: A Time for Betrayal
format: Sean Fennessey, Amanda Dobbins, and Chris Ryan draft the movies of 1998 across six categories, then Sean talks to Fran Kranz about his directorial debut, Mass.

segments.heading: 1998, before the draft
segments.note: the box office, the Oscars, and the mood of the year

films.title: Blues Brothers 2000
films.note: Sean's real origin story: his uncle flew him to L.A. for his 16th birthday to attend the premiere and after-party, where he met Dan Aykroyd, John Goodman, and Joe Morton. The movie itself is 'quite quite quite a bad movie.'

films.title: A Bug's Life
films.note: No. 4 at the domestic box office this year.

films.title: The Waterboy
films.note: No. 5 at the box office, grouped with The Wedding Singer as 'the Waterboy and the Wedding Singer double year.'

films.title: Dr. Dolittle
films.note: No. 6 at the box office.

films.title: Rush Hour
films.note: No. 7 at the box office.

films.title: Deep Impact
films.note: No. 8 at the box office, released the same year as its disaster-movie twin, Armageddon.

films.title: Godzilla
films.note: No. 9 at the box office; barely discussed all episode, and Sean calls it 'quite bad,' though it was marketed as a huge event.

films.title: Patch Adams
films.note: No. 10 at the box office; Amanda saw it in theaters at 13 and cites it as one of the year's 'dog shit movies.'

films.title: Primary Colors
films.note: The thinly veiled Bill Clinton campaign roman a clef, based on Joe Klein's anonymously published novel.

films.title: A Simple Plan
films.note: Amanda's example of a movie with 'politics underneath its fingernails.' Also gave Billy Bob Thornton a double Oscar nomination (adapted screenplay and supporting actor) the same year Armageddon made him briefly the most talked-about man in Hollywood.

films.title: The Siege
films.note: Cited as prescient about the war on terror, years before 9/11.

films.title: The X-Files
films.note: What felt like a joke at the time, a movie spun off a TV show, is now the standard Hollywood model.

films.title: What Dreams May Come
films.note: Amanda saw it in theaters at 13 and remembers being 'confused.'

films.title: The Spanish Prisoner
films.note: Sean's joking dig at what 13-year-old Amanda should have been watching instead of Patch Adams.

films.title: Affliction
films.note: Nick Nolte's Oscar-nominated performance; he lost, and per Chris, has still never won.

films.title: Gods and Monsters
films.note: Ian McKellen's Oscar-nominated performance; also a loss, also still no Oscar.

segments.heading: The 1998 Movie Draft

picks.title: The Big Lebowski
picks.note: First overall pick and, he says, the movie he's seen and loved the most from this year: Jeff Bridges, John Turturro, Steve Buscemi, John Goodman.

picks.title: Rounders
picks.note: Taken right out from under Sean, who had it queued for his next pick, prompting a whole bit about betrayal. Damon and Norton in a movie Chris calls 'imprinted on his soul.'

picks.title: Saving Private Ryan
picks.note: The other half of his 'double Damon' round. He calls the Normandy assault and the closing town battle some of the best action filmmaking he's ever seen.

picks.title: The Faculty
picks.note: Robert Rodriguez's alien-teachers riff, with peak Josh Hartnett and a loaded adult cast: Salma Hayek, Famke Janssen, Piper Laurie, Christopher McDonald, Jon Stewart, Robert Patrick.

picks.title: Enemy of the State
picks.note: Tony Scott's surveillance thriller with Will Smith and Gene Hackman; one of his favorite Scott films.

picks.title: Belly
picks.note: Hype Williams's only feature film, with DMX and Nas. Chris says he watched it on VHS loop in 1999 and considers it a formative document of that era of rap.

picks.title: Out of Sight
picks.note: Taken after Chris and Amanda's opening surprises left him without a plan; his third or fourth favorite movie of the year.

picks.title: Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas
picks.note: Watched at 16; Terry Gilliam's Hunter S. Thompson adaptation with Johnny Depp and Benicio del Toro, back when Depp's Thompson fixation was still just a fun fact.

picks.title: The Thin Red Line
picks.note: Malick's disorienting, poetic comeback after two decades away from filmmaking, picked over American History X and Affliction. Not Sean's favorite Malick, but a confirmation of his new, more impressionistic style.

picks.title: Ronin
picks.note: A pure spite pick after Rounders got sniped: a movie Sean has never actually seen, drafted purely off Chris Ryan's years of evangelizing for it. John Frankenheimer, De Niro, Jean Reno, Natascha McElhone.

picks.title: There's Something About Mary
picks.note: Taken here to free up comedy for later picks. Remembered as an event movie, with the earlobe and zipper gags that had audiences 'rolling in the aisles.'

picks.title: Wild Things
picks.note: John McNaughton's sweaty Miami-set noir with Neve Campbell, Denise Richards, Matt Dillon, Kevin Bacon, and Bill Murray; seen by Sean at 16.

picks.title: The Truman Show
picks.note: Seen in theaters at 13; Peter Weir's surveillance parable that both hosts credit with predicting a lot about reality TV and being watched.

picks.title: Rushmore
picks.note: Her first Wes Anderson movie, shown to her early on by a college friend from Houston, where it was filmed. 'Made me realize that not all movies have to be the same.'

picks.title: Shakespeare in Love
picks.note: The Best Picture upset over Saving Private Ryan, powered by a notorious Harvey Weinstein campaign; Amanda still likes the movie despite that history, and despite Judi Dench winning Supporting Actress for under eight minutes of screen time.

picks.title: Armageddon
picks.note: Her first Michael Bay movie, seen at 13; she says she wept and calls it a crash course in romance, death, oil drilling, and Aerosmith all at once.

picks.title: You've Got Mail
picks.note: The movie that inspired the whole 1998 draft in the first place. Nora Ephron's Tom Hanks/Meg Ryan rom-com, her most-rewatched movie ever.

picks.title: Sliding Doors
picks.note: Gwyneth Paltrow's other big role this year, and what Amanda calls 'the number one bad breakup haircut of all time.'

undrafted: How Stella Got Her Groove Back

films.title: The General
films.note: John Boorman's film with an 'incredible' Brendan Gleeson performance, brought up in the same breath as the crew's regret over forgetting Gleeson on their Departed episode.

films.title: Bulworth
films.note: Warren Beatty's satire about a politician who becomes, in Sean's telling, basically Bernie Sanders. Bold racial politics that the hosts agree 'doesn't land the plane,' but a good soundtrack (including 'Ghetto Superstar').

films.title: Desperate Measures
films.note: Barbet Schroeder thriller: a cop (Michael Keaton) needs a bone-marrow donor for his son, and the only match is a convicted murderer (Andy Garcia). Chris reads the logline as an argument for bringing back this kind of B-movie premise.

films.title: American History X
films.note: One of Chris's other Oscar Nominee finalists, alongside Affliction. An earnest, on-the-nose telling of race and family with a famously complicated production between director Tony Kaye and Edward Norton.

films.title: Great Expectations
films.note: Alfonso Cuaron's modern-dress adaptation with Ethan Hawke; the hosts agree the movie itself is mediocre but remember the soundtrack fondly (Grateful Dead, Chris Cornell, Tori Amos, Pulp, Iggy Pop, and a solo Scott Weiland deep cut).

films.title: The Parent Trap
films.note: Nancy Meyers's directorial debut and Lindsay Lohan's breakout; Amanda tried to copy Lohan's haircut from it.

films.title: Vampires
films.note: John Carpenter's film, with James Woods and Daniel Baldwin; Sean rewatched it recently and calls it delightful.

films.title: Half Baked
films.note: Chris says he almost drafted this in comedy over Big Lebowski.

films.title: Hard Rain
films.note: Christian Slater and Morgan Freeman rob a bank during a flood; the hosts hold it up as an example of a mid-budget B-movie premise Hollywood doesn't make anymore.

films.title: The Mask of Zorro
films.note: Chris says he almost took this in Oscar Nominee; Martin Campbell's first film after GoldenEye, with Antonio Banderas.

films.title: Twilight
films.note: Robert Benton's neo-noir with Paul Newman, Susan Sarandon, and Gene Hackman; Chris calls it 'quite good.' Not the vampire movie.

films.title: BASEketball
films.note: Brought up in passing near the end of the recap; Amanda says she's 'not a fan.'

films.title: Mass
films.note: His debut as writer-director: two couples meet years after a school shooting that connects their families, in a single church meeting room. He wrote it after his own daughter was born and the Parkland shooting sent him into two years of reading about mass shootings. He insisted on no flashbacks or cutaways, so the conversation plays out close to real time.

films.title: I, Daniel Blake
films.note: A touchstone for how Mass should look and feel: Ken Loach's unshowy realism, where 'you don't feel him as a director.'

films.title: American Honey
films.note: Not a direct comparison to Mass, but its cinematographer, Robbie Ryan (who also shot I, Daniel Blake), was a reference point for the film's handheld back half.

films.title: My Dinner with Andre
films.note: The one-location, mostly-two-people conversation movie he kept returning to while writing.

films.title: Moonlight
films.note: Its opening shot (stable on the hood of a car, then rising into extended handheld as Mahershala Ali gets out) was the direct model for the moment Mass shifts from static wide shots into handheld.

films.title: Tokyo Story
films.note: Ozu's formal wide shots and doorway framing shaped his decision to withhold the film's first close-up until Martha Plimpton's character speaks.

films.title: Black Jack
films.note: His 'last great thing' seen: a rarely discussed Ken Loach period piece about a boy roaming 19th-century England with a Frenchman who survived his own hanging.

films.title: The Wailing
films.note: His other recent favorite: the genre-bending Korean film he praises for never losing 'truthful human emotion' amid the chaos.

Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas.title: Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas

How Stella Got Her Groove Back.title: How Stella Got Her Groove Back
